text:
City Neurology Service

Metropolitan Neurosciences Centre

Clinic Date: 12 September 2025

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures with longstanding pattern, post-injury headaches, intermittent low mood, musculoskeletal strain from prolonged standing at work.

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily. Clobazam 10 mg nocte. PRN buccal midazolam 10 mg for prolonged events. Omeprazole 20 mg daily. Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily.

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today. He attended unaccompanied. He works at a busy metro station gate line where prolonged standing and crowd surges are part of the environment. He reports that shift patterns and overnight duties can aggravate fatigue, and he occasionally needs to step off the concourse when prodromal symptoms occur. He wears a body-worn alarm linked to station control and has completed workplace first-aid briefings with his colleagues. He denies recent injuries at work but notes occasional shoulder and neck stiffness after sudden tensing episodes. There is no current alcohol or recreational drug use. He is a non-smoker.

Seizures: Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. He keeps a digital diary, and station incident logs corroborate occasional brief events on duty, typically without prolonged post-ictal confusion. He reports reliable adherence to medication, and has set phone reminders aligned with shift times. No recent emergency department attendances.

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. No lateralising weakness. Gait normal; mild trapezius tenderness to palpation. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable.

Investigations: Most recent bloods from May 2025 showed normal FBC, U&E, LFTs; vitamin D borderline low at 23 nmol/L (supplementation ongoing). Prior MRI (2022) unchanged; routine EEGs non-diagnostic. No new imaging requested today.

Assessment: Persistent seizure burden without clear recent escalation. Workplace risks mitigated through reasonable adjustments, but ongoing nocturnal events and clustered drop episodes continue to pose safety concerns, particularly around crowd surges.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine and clobazam at current doses; reinforce strict adherence around shift changes.
- Buccal midazolam plan unchanged; station supervisors retain training and access as documented.
- Occupational health letter to confirm current safety measures and recommend rota stability where feasible to reduce sleep deprivation.
- Physiotherapy for neck/shoulder strain related to tonic episodes and posture during long shifts.
- Check serum vitamin D, lamotrigine level, and routine safety bloods in primary care; we will review results via clinic mail.
- Seizure diary to continue; advise filming typical events where safe for semiology review.
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if injury, status, or pattern change.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week

Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit numeric seizure frequencies. The letter states: 'he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week' (explicit numeric frequency). It also mentions 'drop attacks occurring in batches' (clusters without a numeric rate) and 'tonic seizures during both day and night' (no numeric rate). Per the rule to select the highest frequency, use the explicit numeric rate available. There is no higher or additional numeric frequency provided to sum. Therefore, the current seizure frequency label is 3 per week.
seizure_frequency_number: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week
reference: Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change
